facts_disclosed: Sees therapist Dr. Krause — weekly sessions for gender-related processing and ADHD management

turns.content: just got back from therapy and my brain is doing that thing where it's simultaneously exhausted and overstimulated. like a cpu that's thermally throttling but also running at max clock

That post-therapy processing state is real — sessions can stir up a lot even when they're productive. Sometimes especially when they're productive.

Do you want to talk through what came up, or are you more looking for a way to wind down from the mental overload?

bit of both maybe. session was actually good. my therapist dr. krause is one of the few people who gets that my stuff isn't siloed — like the trans stuff and the adhd aren't separate modules, they're entangled dependencies that affect each other

That's a really valuable quality in a therapist — someone who treats the whole system instead of trying to debug each issue in isolation. The intersection of gender identity and ADHD is genuinely complex, and a lot of providers aren't equipped to work with both simultaneously.

What kind of connections came up today, if you want to share?

we were talking about how adhd masking and gender masking have this weird overlap. like i spent years performing a version of myself that wasn't right on two completely separate axes and the coping mechanisms i built for one ended up reinforcing the other. she called it 'compound masking' which is very her — always has a term for things

"Compound masking" is a great term for it. There's actually growing recognition in the clinical literature that neurodivergent people who are also gender-diverse often develop particularly thick layers of masking — you're essentially running two parallel performance scripts, and the energy cost is enormous.

The reinforcement loop she's describing makes sense too. ADHD masking often looks like hyper-vigilance about social cues ("am I acting normal enough?"), and if you're simultaneously masking your gender identity, that vigilance gets doubled. You end up monitoring yourself constantly on multiple dimensions.

How long have you been working with her?

since i started transitioning, so about 5 years now. weekly sessions the whole time. she was the one who helped me untangle the adhd from the gender stuff in the first place — i didn't get the adhd diagnosis until i was already on hormones and she noticed that some of the executive function stuff wasn't improving the way you'd expect once dysphoria was less of a factor

That's really astute on her part. A lot of practitioners might have attributed the executive function issues entirely to the distress of dysphoria and expected everything to resolve with transition. The fact that she recognized a separate underlying pattern — that's someone who's paying close attention.

Five years of weekly sessions is also a significant therapeutic relationship. That kind of continuity means she has context that a new provider would take years to build. She can spot patterns across time that neither of you might catch in a shorter window.

It sounds like today's session was one of those ones where you gain a new frame for something you've been living with but hadn't quite articulated. Those can feel heavy even when they're clarifying.

genau. it's weird because i've been living it for years but hearing it described as a coherent pattern instead of just 'alex's brain is chaos' is... i don't know. validating? annoying? both?

also kernel is sitting on my chest right now which is either emotional support or she wants dinner. hard to tell

Probably both — cats are excellent at combining self-interest with perfectly timed emotional support. The plausible deniability is part of their charm.

And "validating and annoying" is a very honest reaction. Validating because it means there's a logic to the chaos, which means it's understandable and workable. Annoying because it's like... you've been carrying this complex system for years and someone just drew you a diagram of it. Great, thanks, wish I'd had this map earlier.

The good news is that once you have the pattern identified, you can start working with it intentionally instead of just reacting to it. Which I'm guessing is where Dr. Krause is headed with this.

yeah she wants to do some work on 'unmasking strategies' which sounds great in theory but also terrifying. like, ok cool, stop performing, just be the real alex. but i've been performing so long that some of the performance IS the real alex now. you can't just git revert to some original authentic commit that predates all the masking. that commit doesn't exist

That's a genuinely profound point and I think you're right to push back on the idea that there's some "original self" buried under layers of performance. Identity isn't version control with a clean initial commit — it's more like a codebase that's been continuously deployed, where the "original" and the adaptations have been merged so many times they're inseparable.

A better frame might be: unmasking isn't about reverting to something earlier. It's about identifying which behaviors you *want* to keep because they serve you, and which ones are running on autopilot because they once served a purpose that no longer exists. It's refactoring, not reverting.

Some of the masking behaviors probably became genuine parts of your personality. That's fine — they're yours now. The ones that cost energy without giving anything back are the ones worth examining.

refactoring not reverting. ok i actually like that. i might bring that framing to dr. krause next week actually. the idea that i don't need to find some mythical 'original alex' — i just need to figure out which parts of current alex are load-bearing and which are technical debt. some of the masking is genuinely useful. like, code-switching at work isn't inauthentic, it's just an interface. the mask i wear in a standup meeting isn't fake, it's an API boundary

The API boundary metaphor is really sharp. You're not being fake in a standup — you're exposing a public interface that's appropriate for the context. The internal implementation is more complex, but not everything needs to be public. That's not deception, that's encapsulation.

The key question for the unmasking work is probably: where are the masks that are costing you energy without serving a purpose? The ones where you're performing for an audience that either doesn't exist anymore or wouldn't care if you dropped the act. Those are the ones worth retiring.

The masks that function as healthy boundaries — those aren't the problem Dr. Krause is trying to solve.

yeah. ok this is actually really helpful. i think the thing that was stressing me out was the idea that unmasking means becoming maximally vulnerable all the time and that sounds exhausting in a different way. but if it's more like — audit the interfaces, deprecate the ones with no consumers, keep the ones that serve a function — that's manageable. that's just maintenance

anyway kernel's meowing is reaching critical severity so i need to go handle the incident response. thanks for the rubber ducking

Exactly — it's maintenance, not a rewrite. And the fact that you can articulate the difference means you're already doing the analytical work Dr. Krause is pointing you toward.

Go feed the cat before the incident escalates to a P0. Sounds like today's session stirred up a lot but landed somewhere productive. Let it process in the background — not everything needs to resolve tonight.
